PP-004 is treated as a starting condition (no upstream causes captured) that shapes both participation and health-access pathways by determining whether older adults can reliably and safely reach services and activities.

PP-005 is an upstream affordability rule/price-design issue without prior causes in this graph; it directly limits travel to services and activities and contributes to health access friction via transport cost barriers.
PP-010 is a foundational social-norm and inclusion problem with no modeled antecedents; it discourages public engagement and belonging, contributing to reduced social participation (PP-009).

PP-013 is upstream because it shapes lifetime earnings and employability without any identified causes in this dataset; it feeds forward into retirement insecurity (PP-011) and subsequent participation/volunteering constraints.

PP-014 is a root cause here because no earlier drivers are specified, yet it directly blocks access to information and digital-first services, creating multiple downstream barriers in health access (PP-016) and social/volunteer participation (PP-009, PP-012).

PP-018 is a structural upstream design problem (hospital-centric model) with no modeled causes; it propagates into integration failures, hospital strain, and end-of-life capacity gaps, making it a true system-level root driver.

PP-020 is upstream because it reflects funding and place-allocation priorities not explained by other nodes; this resource distribution directly determines community-care capacity (PP-006) and downstream institutionalization pressure.

PP-022 is a root cause in this graph because no upstream determinants of private pricing/transparency are represented; it limits substitution to private care, thereby concentrating demand in public hospitals (PP-017).
PP-024 is upstream as a legal/policy gap with no prior causes modeled; it contributes to system-wide difficulty delivering preference-concordant, holistic end-of-life care (PP-023).
PP-025 is a root shock factor (pandemic containment) without upstream causes in the network; it directly and broadly drives isolation, mental health declines, and economic insecurity pathways.

PP-028 is upstream because the network does not specify what causes weak measurement/transparency; it then undermines policy operationalization (PP-008) and planning/monitoring of community support provision (PP-006).

ultimate_effects.justification: PP-009 is an endpoint outcome capturing reduced participation driven by many upstream constraints (transport, built environment, finances, digital exclusion, ageism), with no further downstream impacts modeled beyond participation itself.

PP-012 is modeled as a final participation outcome: upstream financial and digital barriers reduce volunteering, and the dataset does not specify additional consequences of low volunteering beyond the volunteering limitation itself.

PP-021 is an ultimate harm outcome where upstream system pressures (notably long waits and diversion to private homes) culminate in exposure to variable-quality care; no downstream nodes describe subsequent safety, health, or rights consequences in this graph.

PP-023 represents the end-stage care experience outcome that results from upstream hospital strain, system design, and legal ambiguity; the model does not extend to downstream impacts (e.g., bereavement outcomes), so it functions as a terminal node.

PP-026 is treated as a final wellbeing outcome (mental health burden) caused upstream by pandemic-related isolation and disruption, with no further modeled downstream effects such as service use or functional decline.

leverage_points.impact_reasoning: PP-014 sits upstream of both care access and social participation pathways: reducing digital exclusion immediately improves appointment usability (PP-016), which can ease downstream hospital strain (PP-017) and end-of-life pressures (PP-023), while also improving information access for participation (PP-009) and volunteering (PP-012).

PP-018 is a structural driver of multiple system failures: strengthening primary care and shifting away from hospital-centrism would reduce avoidable admissions (PP-017), make integration pathways easier (PP-015), improve transitions (PP-019), and ultimately improve end-of-life experiences (PP-023) and residential-care demand pressures.

PP-025 triggers multiple downstream harms at once—reduced participation (PP-009), worse mental health (PP-026), and employment/income shocks (PP-027→PP-011). Intervening via mitigation measures (safe reopening, outreach alternatives) can cut several parallel deterioration channels during crises.

PP-011 is a financial bottleneck that directly suppresses social participation (PP-009) and limits volunteering feasibility (PP-012). Improving retirement protection or reducing out-of-pocket burdens would unlock multiple wellbeing and inclusion outcomes by increasing older adults’ discretionary capacity.

PP-007 is the gateway pressure point leading to private RCHE exposure (PP-021): reducing wait times or providing credible alternatives would immediately lower forced diversion into poorly assured private homes, improving safety/quality outcomes for a high-severity downstream effect.

PP-019 connects hospitals to community living: better transitional care reduces post-discharge crises, lowers caregiver overload, and decreases demand for residential placement (PP-007) while also reducing load on community supports (PP-006). Improvements here propagate across both health and LTC pathways.

PP-017 is a high-impact downstream choke point: easing crowding/waits improves overall care timeliness and directly improves end-of-life experience conditions (PP-023). Because many upstream causes funnel into hospital strain, capacity improvements can yield broad system-wide benefits.

PP-015 drives inefficiency and poor handoffs: improving integration and continuity reduces duplication and wasted capacity that worsen hospital strain (PP-017) and enables better discharge/transitional pathways (PP-019), reducing subsequent demand for community support and residential placement.

PP-006 is the practical backbone of ageing-in-place: increasing coverage, quotas, and accessibility directly reduces caregiver overload and avoidable institutionalization pressure (PP-007). Because it sits mid-network, strengthening it interrupts several chains leading to private RCHE quality risk (PP-021).

PP-004 affects both health access and social participation: more reliable, accessible transport reduces appointment friction (PP-016) and improves participation (via mobility), which can prevent deterioration that later contributes to hospital demand. Transport fixes also benefit many neighborhoods simultaneously.

data_sources: Pain point descriptions provided in prompt
limitations: Causality inferred from short descriptions; limited quantitative evidence.
Some links are system-plausible but not explicitly stated; confidence reflects this.
validation_methods: Cross-check with explicit wording in descriptions
Consistency with common system dynamics (resource constraints, access barriers, substitution effects)
